FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 3498 U.S. adolescents aged 9-11 years from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as generic total composite (hours/day) from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey covering TV, videos, games, texting, video chat, social media combined. No separately reported phone/SM coefficient. Violates FT-IC2 requirement for specific smartphone OR social media variable.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Obesity, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; does not reach criteria check for FT-EC7
notes: Paper measures generic screen time composite without separate phone/SM coefficient. FT-IC2 fails on composite-only exclusion (per E2: phone/SM-specific row/column extractable required, not whole-composite only).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 7445 adolescents aged 9-10 years from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at baseline (2016-2018)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as composite across multiple modalities (TV, video games, social media, video chatting, etc.) without separately extractable smartphone or social media-specific coefficient. Results report only aggregate screen time associations. Violates FT-IC2 requirement.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Childhood Obesity (Print), October 2025

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; does not reach criteria check for FT-EC7
notes: Paper reports screen time as composite across TV/movies, videos, games, texting, video chat, social media. No separately extractable phone/SM coefficient. FT-IC2 fails per E2 (whole-composite only, not separately measured phone/SM modality).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 791 Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study participants, Mage=14.12, 53% female, with EARS passive sensing data

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone application use measured via EARS (Effortless Assessment Research System) passive sensing; reported by time window: daytime/evening (6AM-7:59PM), late-evening (8PM-9:59PM), late-night (10PM-5:59AM). Specific smartphone exposure variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models with multiple statistical tests: daytime/evening app use predicted reduced exercise, sedentary time, daily steps; late-night use predicted reduced sleep, delayed sleep onset/waking, increased sedentary time, reduced steps. P<.001 reported throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized beta coefficients reported: daytime/evening β=-0.21 to -0.07 (P<.001); late-evening β=-0.05 to -0.02 (P<.001); late-night β=-0.16 to -0.27 (P<.001). Within-person coefficients: daytime/evening β=-0.22 to -0.07 (P<.001); late-evening β=-0.05 to -0.02 (P<.001); late-night β=-0.16 to -0.24 (P≤.002).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective sensor-based study with mixed-effects models examining daily associations between smartphone use and sleep/physical activity over 3 weeks

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Psychiatry Research, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone use is independent variable; sleep, physical activity, steps are dependent variables. Cross-sectional daily-level associations. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (phone/SM as DV).
notes: Null results reported with full statistical coefficients (E11 applies). Uses passive sensing (EARS) which provides objective smartphone measurement. Within-person and between-person effects examined; within-person remained significant.
